title: Hiring and Managing Staff Under Lithuanian Labour Code
subtitle: Employment law here is specific. Get the basics right from day one.

sections.heading: Lithuanian Labour Code Basics
sections.body:
The Darbo kodeksas (Lithuanian Labour Code) governs all employment relationships. It was significantly reformed in 2017 and continues to be updated. As a cafe owner, you do not need to read all 256 articles — but you need to understand the fundamentals.

**Core rules:**

- **Written contract mandatory.** Every employee must have a written employment contract signed before they start work. Verbal agreements are not legally valid.
- **Maximum working week: 40 hours.** Overtime is permitted but capped at 8 hours per week and must be compensated at 1.5x (first 2 hours) or 2x (thereafter).
- **Probation period: maximum 3 months.** During probation, either party can terminate with 3 working days' notice. After probation, notice periods extend significantly.
- **Night work: 22:00–06:00.** Requires at least 1.5x pay. If your cafe opens at 06:00, the barista arriving at 05:30 for setup is technically in night-work territory.
- **Rest periods.** At least 11 consecutive hours between shifts. At least 35 consecutive hours per week (typically the weekend). A break of at least 30 minutes during any shift over 6 hours.

**Common cafe-specific issues:**
- Split shifts (morning peak + evening) are legal but must respect the 11-hour rest rule
- Students on part-time contracts have additional restrictions on hours during term time
- Seasonal contracts are permitted for terrace-season staff (fixed-term, clearly defined)
- Cash-in-hand employment is illegal and carries severe penalties for the employer

**Get a lawyer.** For your first hire, spend €200–300 on a Lithuanian employment lawyer to review your contract template and advise on your specific situation. This one-time cost prevents expensive mistakes later.

sections.heading: The Employment Contract
sections.body:
Use T214-01 (Employment Contract Template) as a starting point, but always have it reviewed by a Lithuanian employment lawyer.

**Mandatory contract elements:**

| Element | What to Include |
|---------|----------------|
| Employer details | Company name, code, registered address |
| Employee details | Full name, personal code (asmens kodas), address |
| Job title and description | Specific role: barista, shift lead, manager |
| Start date | First day of employment |
| Duration | Indefinite or fixed-term (with reason and end date) |
| Workplace | Cafe address |
| Working hours | Hours per week, shift pattern if applicable |
| Probation period | Up to 3 months (specify or it defaults to none) |
| Salary | Gross monthly amount, payment frequency and method |
| Annual leave | Minimum 20 working days (can offer more) |
| Notice period | Per Labour Code (varies by tenure) |
| Collective agreement | Reference if applicable (rare in small cafes) |

**Contract types for cafes:**

- **Indefinite (neterminuota):** Standard for core team. No end date. Strongest employee protections.
- **Fixed-term (terminuota):** For seasonal staff (summer terrace), project work, or maternity cover. Must specify the reason and end date. Maximum 2 years, renewable once.
- **Part-time:** Specify exact hours per week. Same rights as full-time, pro-rated.

**Before the first shift:**
1. Contract signed by both parties
2. SODRA notification submitted (within 1 working hour of start)
3. Employee's bank details collected for salary payment
4. Copy of ID verified and filed
5. Food safety training confirmed or scheduled

Never let someone work a "trial shift" without a contract. In Lithuania, work without a contract exposes you to fines of €500–6,000 per violation.

sections.heading: Leave Entitlements
sections.body:
Lithuanian leave entitlements are generous by European standards. Budget for them — they are not optional.

**Annual leave:**
- Minimum: **20 working days** per year (4 calendar weeks)
- Enhanced: 25 working days for employees under 18, single parents of children under 14, or employees with disabilities
- Extended: 38–58 working days for specific categories (hazardous work conditions — unlikely for cafes)
- Accrual: Leave accrues from the first day of employment
- Carry-over: Unused leave must be taken within 1 year; it does not expire automatically but should not be allowed to accumulate indefinitely

**Maternity leave:** 126 calendar days (70 days before and 56 days after birth). Paid by SODRA at 77.58% of compensatory salary.

**Paternity leave:** 30 calendar days within the first 3 months after birth. Paid by SODRA at 77.58%.

**Parental leave:** Either parent can take leave until the child reaches age 3. SODRA pays benefits: 77.58% of salary until child is 1, then 54.31% until child is 2 (if chosen). The third year is unpaid but the job is protected.

**Sick leave:** Employer pays for the first 2 working days at 62.06% of average salary. From day 3, SODRA takes over at 62.06% (first 30 days) then 68.27% (days 31–120). Maximum sick leave: 120 days per year.

**Unpaid leave:** Employee can request up to 14 calendar days per year. Employer can refuse only with good business reason.

**Practical impact for a cafe:**
With a team of 6, at any given time 1 person may be on annual leave. In summer, when you need maximum staffing for the terrace, leave requests compete with business need. Set a clear policy: leave requests submitted 4 weeks ahead, maximum 1 person on leave at a time (or 2 if team is larger), summer peak weeks blocked or limited.

sections.heading: Minimum Wage and Pay Structure
sections.body:
**Minimum wage (2025):** €924 gross per month (full-time, 40 hours/week). This translates to approximately €5.78 gross per hour. Check the current rate annually — it increases regularly.

**Typical cafe wages in Lithuania (2025–2026 estimates):**

| Role | Gross Monthly | Net Monthly (approx) | Notes |
|------|--------------|---------------------|-------|
| Barista (entry) | €924–1,100 | €680–790 | Minimum wage to +20% |
| Experienced barista | €1,100–1,300 | €790–920 | 1+ year specialty experience |
| Shift lead | €1,300–1,500 | €920–1,050 | Operational responsibility |
| Cafe manager | €1,500–2,000 | €1,050–1,380 | Full site management |

*Net amounts are approximate and depend on individual NPD (tax-free amount) calculation.*

**Pay structure rules:**
- Salary paid at least twice per month (advance by 16th, remainder by end of month) unless employee agrees in writing to monthly payment
- Paid by bank transfer to the employee's account (cash salary is technically possible but creates SODRA/tax complications)
- Payslip required with every payment showing gross, deductions, and net
- Overtime: 1.5x for first 2 hours, 2x beyond. Night premium: at least 1.5x for 22:00–06:00
- Weekend premium: at least 2x if not part of the regular schedule

**Tips in Lithuanian cafes:**
Tips are increasingly common, especially in tourist areas. There is no formal tronc system like in some countries. Best practice:
- Cash tips: typically kept by the individual or pooled and split at end of shift
- Card tips: if your POS supports it, distribute monthly with payroll
- Set a tip policy in writing so the team knows the rules
- Technically, tips are taxable income — in practice, cash tips are rarely declared. Card tips processed through payroll should be taxed correctly.

**Competitive positioning:** In Vilnius's competitive barista market, paying €100–200 above minimum wage for a good barista is worth it. Turnover costs far more than the wage premium.

sections.heading: Termination and Disciplinary Process
sections.body:
Letting someone go in Lithuania is more structured than in many countries. Understand the rules before you need them.

**During probation (first 3 months):**
- Either party can terminate with 3 working days' written notice
- No reason required
- No severance pay
- This is the window to assess fit — use it actively

**After probation — employer-initiated termination:**

**For cause (employee fault):** Gross misconduct (theft, violence, intoxication at work, serious safety violation). Can be immediate dismissal with written notice and documented evidence. No severance.

**Without cause (employer decision):** Redundancy, restructuring, or simply not needing the role. Requires:
- 1 month written notice (2 months if employed 1+ years, 3 months if 3+ years)
- Severance pay: 2 months' average salary (if employed less than 1 year), increasing with tenure
- Notification to the local Employment Service if making 10+ employees redundant

**Disciplinary process:**
1. Written warning for first offence (document the behaviour specifically)
2. Written request for the employee's explanation (they have 5 working days to respond)
3. Second written warning if behaviour repeats
4. After 2 warnings within 12 months, termination for cause is possible
5. All steps must be documented and timed correctly

**What you cannot do:**
- Fire someone for being pregnant, on parental leave, or on sick leave
- Fire someone for union membership or whistleblowing
- Dismiss without following the documented procedure (even if the employee deserves it)
- Use probation termination as a backdoor after the probation period has expired

**Practical advice:** The disciplinary conversation is hard but necessary. Document everything from the first conversation. "I spoke to [name] on [date] about [specific behaviour]. They acknowledged the issue and agreed to [specific improvement]." This protects you if the situation escalates.

sections.heading: SODRA Registration and Ongoing Obligations
sections.body:
SODRA registration is not optional and the timing is strict.

**When you hire someone:**

1. **Before they start:** Sign the employment contract
2. **Within 1 working hour of their scheduled start:** Submit notification to SODRA through the EDAS portal (elektroninė draudėjų aptarnavimo sistema)
3. **What you submit:** Employee's personal code (asmens kodas), start date, contract type, working hours, salary
4. **Confirmation:** SODRA issues a confirmation number. Save it.

**The 1-hour rule is enforced.** If an inspector visits and finds an employee working without SODRA registration, the fine starts at €500 and can reach €6,000 for repeat offences. The employee also loses social insurance coverage for the unregistered period — if they are injured at work, this becomes a serious legal problem for you.

**EDAS portal access:**
Register your business as a draudėjas (insurer) on the EDAS portal. This is a one-time setup. Once registered, you can:
- Submit new employee notifications
- Report employee departures
- Submit monthly contribution reports
- Access contribution histories
- Download certificates and confirmations

**When someone leaves:**
Notify SODRA within 1 working day of the last day of employment. Submit through EDAS with the end date and reason for termination.

**Monthly rhythm:**
- Calculate contributions based on actual gross salaries paid
- Submit monthly report to SODRA by the 15th
- Pay contributions by the 15th
- Reconcile with your payroll records

**Common mistakes:**
- Forgetting to register a trial shift (yes, even a paid trial day requires SODRA notification)
- Late notification for new hires (the 1-hour clock starts at the employee's scheduled start time, not when they actually arrive)
- Not deregistering employees who leave (you continue paying contributions on their behalf)
- Miscalculating contributions when an employee works overtime or receives bonuses

Your accountant should handle SODRA reporting as part of monthly payroll. But verify — check the EDAS portal yourself quarterly to ensure all employees are registered and contributions are current.
